summary: Julia Evans shares a talk she gave at Montreal Python about graphing bike path usage data from Montreal's open data portal. She combined bike counter sensor data with weather data from Canada's National Climate Archive to explore relationships between cycling and weather conditions. The analysis was done using pandas and IPython Notebook, and she links to the viewable notebook. She invites readers to download the notebook and do their own analysis at an upcoming Python Night event.
key_points: Montreal collects bike path usage data via sensors, available as open data
Combining datasets (bike counts + weather) reveals patterns between cycling and conditions like temperature and rain
IPython Notebook and pandas make it easy to share reproducible data analysis that others can run themselves
money_quotes: I showed some graphs of # bikes vs temperature and # bikes vs raininess, using data downloaded from the Canadian National Climate Archive.
If you download it you can run it yourself and do some more complicated analysis.
key_insight: Combining publicly available open datasets with tools like pandas and IPython Notebook makes exploratory data analysis accessible and shareable.
